curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: stretching and compressing graphs (y = a·f(x), y = f(bx))
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Explore how multiplying a function by a changes vertical scale (amplitude) and how replacing x with bx changes horizontal scale (frequency), using sine waves to model rhythms and wellbeing-linked cycles. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

verse_ality.driving_question: What happens to a graph when we stretch or squash it, and how can that help us model real rhythms in wellbeing?

knowing_world.focus: Students investigate vertical and horizontal stretch/compression through y = a·f(x) and y = f(bx), using wave language (amplitude and frequency) to describe how the shape changes.
key_concepts: Describing transformations using correct mathematical language
activities: Explain what happens to a graph when we multiply outputs by a.
Explain what happens to a graph when we replace x with bx.
Use examples with sine waves to describe amplitude and frequency changes.

human_story.focus: Graph stretches and compressions help model biological and human rhythms (heartbeats, sleep cycles) and signals used by engineers and doctors, linking mathematics to health and wellbeing systems.
justice_prompts: How can understanding rhythm and cycles improve human health and wellbeing?
Why might it matter that wellbeing systems are designed to flex with people’s natural rhythms?

expressive_self.focus: Students connect wave transformations to lived experience by sketching and writing about times when life felt stretched or squashed, using visual choices to represent calm and overwhelm.
activities: Sketch a wave that represents your energy through the day.
Write about a time when life felt stretched or squashed.
Use colour (in your own work) to show calm vs overwhelm on a drawn graph.

designed_world.focus: Students use an interactive Web Sketchpad to explore how changing a affects amplitude and changing b affects frequency for y = sin(x), then apply this thinking to an ethical design prompt about digital wellbeing.
tools: Web Sketchpad (embedded): explore y = sin(x) with sliders for a (amplitude) and b (frequency)
activities: Use the slider to change a and describe how the wave changes.
Change b and describe the effect on frequency.
Find settings where the graph feels ‘chaotic’ or ‘calm’ and justify your choices.
design_challenges: Design a digital wellbeing app concept that uses stretched/compressed graphs to show how someone is doing across a week.

wellbeing_prompts: Our bodies and graphs both move in waves—tune into your pattern.

pathways.name: Option 1: Sleep Pattern Model
pathways.focus: Create a model showing a person’s sleep pattern using a stretched sine wave, explaining how the transformation represents the situation.
products: A labelled sine-wave model showing a chosen value of a (amplitude)
A brief explanation linking the graph to sleep rhythm
assessment_criteria: Conceptual Understanding – Explains vertical and horizontal graph changes clearly
Fluency & Accuracy – Accurately uses a and/or b to model transformations
Application to Problems – Connects mathematical waves to real-life cycles
Independence & Reflection – Demonstrates personal insight and creativity

pathways.name: Option 2: Explain Frequency and Amplitude in Wellbeing
pathways.focus: Explain how frequency and amplitude relate to physical or emotional wellbeing, using examples of stretched/compressed waves.
products: At least two example graphs showing different a and/or b values
A clear written or spoken explanation linking amplitude/frequency to wellbeing rhythms
assessment_criteria: Conceptual Understanding – Explains vertical and horizontal graph changes clearly
Fluency & Accuracy – Uses correct terminology (amplitude, frequency, stretch, compression)
Application to Problems – Meaningful link to real-life rhythm/cycle

pathways.name: Option 3: Rhythm Tracker / Wellbeing App Idea
pathways.focus: Design a rhythm tracker or app idea that uses graphs to help someone feel more balanced, using stretches/compressions to represent change over a week.
products: A short design brief or storyboard for the app
Example graphs demonstrating how a and b would be used to represent rhythm changes
A short SDG 3 reflection linking rhythm understanding to wellbeing support
assessment_criteria: Conceptual Understanding – Explains vertical and horizontal graph changes clearly
Fluency & Accuracy – Accurately uses a and b to model transformations
Application to Problems – Connects graphs to wellbeing design
Independence & Reflection – Creativity and personal insight

required: Read and interpret coordinates on a graph
Understand basic function output as y-values
Multiply by integers and simple decimals
Basic familiarity with the idea of a sine wave shape (visual recognition is enough)

builds_toward: Full set of graph transformations (translations, reflections, stretches)
Modelling periodic behaviour in science and technology
